text: welcome to another installment of robotic hernia surgery today we're talking about one of my favorite adjuncts to modern day hernia surgery and that's the madrid modification to the transversus abdominis release this case was performed on a woman with a history of cirrhosis whom we had optimized from a child's class b to a child's class a working with our hepatology team She had a surgical history notable for strangulated umbilical hernia requiring emergency bowel resection and primary repair, along with an episode of laparoscopic lysive adhesions and a transvaginal hysterectomy. These are her representative images from her CT scan. Wide mouth, central defect measuring nine by ten centimeters. She had a rectus width of about nine centimeters. And so we felt she was a good candidate for at least an ETEP approach with a low likelihood to perform TAR.

The surgeon performs an ETEP crossover, initially dissecting on the left side. During the dissection, the peritoneum is inadvertently entered, causing the bowel to fall away. The surgeon quickly develops a retromuscular dissection to manage visualization and address large posterior defects.

The surgeon recruits a portion of the hernia sac for closure after entering the peritoneal cavity, revealing a large posterior defect. The retromuscular dissection is mostly completed, with efforts to mobilize the bladder to aid in bridging the defect and excluding the mesh.

The surgeon drops insufflation and confirms deep paralysis, ensuring adequate retromuscular dissection. The Madrid modification to the transversus abdominis release is demonstrated, showing a muscle-sparing approach that develops a preperitoneal, pre-transversalis dissection without transecting the transversus abdominis muscle. The dissection progresses around the arcuate line, entering the space of Bogros and extending laterally into the retroperitoneum. The preperitoneal plane appears thin, and multiple holes are made during the challenging dissection.

The surgical team carries out a dissection from the bottom up to the subdiaphragmatic region, incising a small segment of the transversus abdominis due to multiple peritoneal holes. The resident performs a tension-free closure of the visceral sac, creating a loose posterior flap that rests on the viscera. The anterior fascial closure is achieved using a permanent V-lock suture, addressing the diastasis and defect closure. The surgical field appears oozy, likely due to the patient's history of portal hypertension and collateralization in the abdominal wall.

The surgeon performs plication of the diastasis, followed by closure of the defect. The transversus abdominis is not released, but the procedure aids in medializing the rectus muscles for reconstructing the linea alba.

The surgeon places a 30x30 Bard soft mesh into the large retromuscular pocket, trimming the corners for a proper fit. Due to tissue oozing, Tisseel is applied, followed by the placement of a retromuscular drain. The procedure concludes with an uncomplicated post-operative course.
